name: Bolivia
tagline: From Tiwanaku on the shores of Lake Titicaca and the Inca highlands to the silver mountain of Potosi that bankrolled an empire, the revolt of Tupac Katari, independence in 1825 named for Simon Bolivar, the loss of the sea to Chile, the National Revolution of 1952, and the indigenous-majority politics of the Morales era and after.
summary: Bolivia's history reaches back to Tiwanaku, the great pre-Hispanic civilisation that flourished near Lake Titicaca between roughly 500 and 900 CE, and to the Aymara lordships and the Inca empire that followed. After the Spanish conquest, the silver of Cerro Rico at Potosi made Upper Peru one of the richest centres of the Spanish empire, worked by mitayos, indigenous men compelled under the mita to labour in mines so deadly the mountain was called the one that eats men. On 6 August 1825 a congress at Chuquisaca proclaimed an independent republic, named Bolivia in honour of Simon Bolivar, with Antonio Jose de Sucre as its first president. The defeat of Bolivia in the War of the Pacific (1879-1883) cost it the Pacific coast and left it landlocked, while the lost Chaco War with Paraguay (1932-1935) helped trigger the National Revolution of 1952; after decades of military rule, democracy returned in 1982, and in 2006 Evo Morales became Bolivia's first indigenous president, beginning an era of indigenous-majority politics that endured through his disputed 2019 election and resignation, the return of the MAS, and the turn to the centre-right in 2025.

founding.label: Proclamation of the Republic of Bolivia
founding.detail: On 6 August 1825 a congress meeting at Chuquisaca (now Sucre) formally proclaimed the independence of the former province of Upper Peru, or Charcas, as a sovereign republic. The new state was named Bolivia in honour of Simon Bolivar, the Liberator, whose campaigns alongside those of Antonio Jose de Sucre had broken Spanish power in the Andes. The decisive military victory had come at the Battle of Ayacucho in Peru on 9 December 1824, where Sucre routed the last great royalist army; he then crossed into Charcas, convened the congress, and became the republic's first constitutionally elected president in 1826. The city of Chuquisaca was later renamed Sucre in his honour, and remains Bolivia's constitutional capital.

quickFacts.label: Founding civilisations
quickFacts.value: Tiwanaku, the Aymara lordships, and the Inca empire

quickFacts.label: Colonial wealth
quickFacts.value: The silver of Cerro Rico at Potosi, worked under the mita

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: Proclaimed 6 August 1825; named for Simon Bolivar

quickFacts.label: First president
quickFacts.value: Antonio Jose de Sucre, victor of Ayacucho

quickFacts.label: Became landlocked
quickFacts.value: After defeat in the War of the Pacific (1879-1883)

eras.title: Tiwanaku, the Aymara, and the Inca
eras.standfirst: On the high Altiplano around Lake Titicaca rose Tiwanaku, one of the great civilisations of the ancient Andes; after its decline the Aymara lordships dominated the highlands until they in turn were absorbed into the expanding Inca empire.
body: High on the Altiplano at some 3,850 metres above sea level, near the southern shore of Lake Titicaca, the city of Tiwanaku became the spiritual and political centre of one of the most important civilisations of the pre-Hispanic Andes. Beginning as a small settlement, it grew into a planned monumental capital that reached its apogee between roughly 500 and 900 CE, dominating a wide swathe of the southern Andes through religion, agriculture and trade rather than by conquest alone. Its great stone gateways, sunken courts and carved monoliths still stand on the plain, and in 2000 UNESCO inscribed Tiwanaku on the World Heritage List as a testimony to a culture distinct from every other empire of the ancient Americas.

After Tiwanaku declined around the twelfth century, the densely populated lands around Lake Titicaca passed to a cluster of Aymara-speaking lordships, herding peoples who built fortified settlements and contested the highlands among themselves. These Aymara kingdoms were the dominant power of the region when, in the fifteenth century, the Inca empire of Cuzco expanded southward into the area the Spanish would later call Charcas. The Inca incorporated the Aymara lands into their realm, the Tahuantinsuyu, but allowed the conquered peoples to keep their own language and identity, so that Aymara survived alongside the Quechua of the Inca.

Under Inca rule the highland economy was organised around the state: terraced fields, herds of llamas and alpacas, and a system of labour obligation, the mit'a, by which communities owed rotating service to the state on roads, fields and public works. This Andean tradition of compulsory rotational labour, and the dense indigenous population of the highlands, would later be seized upon and transformed by the Spanish into a brutal system of forced mine labour. When Francisco Pizarro's expedition shattered the Inca state in the early 1530s, the peoples of present-day Bolivia were drawn, through Cuzco, into the catastrophe of the conquest.

events.title: Tiwanaku reaches its apogee
events.summary: The city of Tiwanaku near Lake Titicaca flourishes as the spiritual and political centre of a powerful Andean culture, building the monumental complex later inscribed by UNESCO.

events.title: The Aymara lordships
events.summary: After Tiwanaku's decline, Aymara-speaking kingdoms dominate the highlands around Lake Titicaca until the rise of the Inca.

events.title: Inca conquest of the highlands
events.summary: The Inca empire expands south into the region the Spanish would call Charcas, absorbing the Aymara lands while permitting them their language and identity.

pullquote.text: Its monumental remains testify to the cultural and political significance of a civilisation distinct from any of the other pre-Hispanic empires of the Americas.

eras.title: Conquest, the Silver of Potosi, and Charcas
eras.standfirst: The Spanish conquest folded the Andean highlands into the empire as Charcas, or Upper Peru; the discovery of silver at Cerro Rico made Potosi one of the wealthiest cities on earth, its mines fed by the forced labour of indigenous mitayos.
body: Following the fall of the Inca, the Spanish organised the highlands of present-day Bolivia as the Audiencia of Charcas, also known as Upper Peru, within the Viceroyalty of Peru and answering ultimately to Lima. The region's destiny was transformed in 1545 by the discovery of vast deposits of silver in the Cerro Rico, the rich mountain, above Potosi. Within a few decades Potosi had become one of the largest cities in the world, with a population that swelled past one hundred thousand, and the single most important source of silver for the Spanish crown. The wealth of the Cerro Rico flowed across the Atlantic to underwrite the wars and ambitions of the Spanish empire.

That wealth was wrung from the labour of the indigenous population. In the 1570s the viceroy Francisco de Toledo systematised a coercive labour draft, the mita, adapting the old Inca rotational service into a system that compelled native men from highland districts across the Andes to spend long periods working in the mines and the mercury refineries of Potosi. Conditions were lethal: men were sent deep underground and worked the silver ore in a process using poisonous mercury, and so many indigenous workers and enslaved Africans perished that the Cerro Rico became known as the mountain that eats men. UNESCO, which inscribed the City of Potosi on the World Heritage List in 1987, recognises it as the supreme witness to this industrial silver complex and the human cost it carried.

Around the mines grew a colonial society of immense inequality, in which a small Spanish and creole elite ruled over an indigenous majority bound by tribute, the mita, and the demands of the Catholic Church. Potosi made the fortunes of merchants and the crown alike, while its silver helped knit together a global economy stretching to Europe and Asia. Yet by the eighteenth century the richest veins were exhausted, the boom faded, and the accumulated grievances of the indigenous communities, crushed by tribute and forced labour, were building toward an explosion that would shake the whole southern Andes.

events.title: Spanish conquest of Charcas
events.summary: Spanish forces subdue the highlands of Upper Peru, organising them as the Audiencia of Charcas within the Viceroyalty of Peru.

events.title: Silver discovered at Cerro Rico
events.summary: The discovery of immense silver deposits in the mountain above Potosi sets off a boom that makes the city one of the largest and richest in the world.

events.title: Toledo systematises the mita
events.summary: Viceroy Francisco de Toledo organises the mita, a forced labour draft that compels indigenous men across the Andes to work the deadly mines and mercury refineries of Potosi.

events.title: Potosi feeds the Spanish empire
events.summary: Potosi's silver becomes the principal source of bullion for the Spanish crown, financing its empire while consuming the lives of indigenous mitayos.

figures.name: Francisco de Toledo
figures.blurb: Spanish viceroy who reorganised the colonial economy of the Andes in the 1570s and systematised the mita labour draft that supplied forced indigenous workers to the silver mines of Potosi.

pullquote.text: So many indigenous people and enslaved Africans died in its mines that the Cerro Rico became known as the mountain that eats men.
pullquote.attribution: On the forced labour of Potosi

eras.title: The Revolt of Tupac Katari and Independence
eras.standfirst: The great Andean rebellion of 1780-1781 brought the Aymara leader Tupac Katari to lay siege to La Paz; a generation later the wars of independence, won at Ayacucho by Sucre, gave birth in 1825 to the Republic of Bolivia.
body: In 1780 the southern Andes erupted in the largest indigenous rebellion the Spanish empire had yet faced. It began in Peru under Tupac Amaru II, a descendant of the Inca, and spread across the highlands into Upper Peru and beyond. In the lands around La Paz the revolt found its own leader in Julian Apasa, an Aymara who took the name Tupac Katari. In 1781 he raised an army said to number tens of thousands and laid siege to La Paz, holding the city under blockade for months. The rising was eventually broken by royalist reinforcements, and Tupac Katari was captured and brutally executed; long treated as a minor episode, he and his wife Bartolina Sisa are today honoured as martyrs of indigenous resistance.

The independence of Spanish America was set in motion when Napoleon's invasion of Spain in 1808 threw the colonies into crisis. Upper Peru was among the first to stir: uprisings at Chuquisaca and La Paz in 1809 are remembered as early cries for freedom in the Americas. But the region, ringed by royalist strongholds and the silver wealth the crown was determined to hold, became a long and bloody battleground, and full independence came only at the very end of the continental wars, carried from outside by the armies of Simon Bolivar and Antonio Jose de Sucre advancing south from Gran Colombia.

The decisive blow fell at the Battle of Ayacucho in the Peruvian highlands on 9 December 1824, where Sucre destroyed the last great Spanish army in South America and effectively secured the independence of the continent. Sucre then led his forces into Charcas, expelled the remaining royalists, and convened a congress. On 6 August 1825 that congress proclaimed an independent republic, choosing to name it Bolivia in honour of the Liberator, Simon Bolivar, who drafted its first constitution. Sucre became the new nation's first constitutionally elected president in 1826, and the colonial capital of Chuquisaca was later renamed Sucre in his memory.

events.title: Tupac Katari besieges La Paz
events.summary: The Aymara leader Tupac Katari, part of the great Andean rebellion begun by Tupac Amaru II, lays siege to La Paz before being captured and executed.

events.title: Uprisings at Chuquisaca and La Paz
events.summary: Revolts in Upper Peru in 1809, sparked by the Spanish crisis under Napoleon, are remembered among the first independence movements in the Americas.

events.title: Battle of Ayacucho
events.summary: Antonio Jose de Sucre routs the last great royalist army at Ayacucho, sealing the independence of Spanish South America and opening the way to liberate Charcas.

events.title: Republic of Bolivia proclaimed
events.summary: A congress at Chuquisaca declares the independence of Upper Peru as a republic named Bolivia in honour of Simon Bolivar, with Sucre soon its first president.

figures.name: Tupac Katari
figures.blurb: Born Julian Apasa, he led the Aymara of the La Paz region in the great Andean rebellion of 1781, besieging the city for months before his capture and execution. He is now honoured as a symbol of indigenous resistance.

figures.name: Antonio Jose de Sucre
figures.role: Liberator and first president of Bolivia
figures.blurb: Bolivar's foremost lieutenant, victor of the Battle of Ayacucho in 1824, who liberated Charcas and became Bolivia's first constitutionally elected president, embarking on social and economic reforms before resigning in 1828.

pullquote.text: They chose to name the new republic Bolivia, in honour of the Liberator, Simon Bolivar.

eras.title: Lost Wars, the Sea, and the Tin Republic
eras.standfirst: A turbulent young republic lost its Pacific coast to Chile in the War of the Pacific and was left landlocked; an economy built on tin enriched a few mine owners, and a disastrous war with Paraguay over the Chaco shattered the old order.
body: The first decades of the republic were marked by instability, caudillo rule and frequent coups, and by repeated conflict with its neighbours. The gravest blow came in the War of the Pacific. In 1879 Chile, in dispute with Bolivia over the nitrate-rich Atacama desert, occupied the Bolivian port of Antofagasta; Bolivia declared war and was joined by its ally Peru. Chilean forces won command of the sea and overran the coast, and in the settlement that followed Bolivia ceded its entire Pacific littoral, the province of Antofagasta, to Chile. The loss of the sea, confirmed by treaty in 1904, left Bolivia landlocked, a wound to the national psyche that endures and that the country has never ceased to contest.

As silver had once defined the colony, tin came to define the modern republic. From the late nineteenth century Bolivia's tin mines, concentrated in the hands of a few great magnates known as the tin barons, made the country one of the world's leading producers and tied its fortunes to a single export. The mineral wealth flowed to a narrow elite while the largely indigenous mine workers laboured in harsh conditions, and the gap between the mining and landowning oligarchy and the impoverished indigenous majority widened into the central fault line of Bolivian society.

That tension was brought to breaking point by another catastrophic war. Between 1932 and 1935 Bolivia fought Paraguay over the Gran Chaco, a hot lowland wilderness wrongly believed to hold great reserves of oil, and to which a landlocked Bolivia looked for a river route to the Atlantic. Despite its larger population and better-armed forces, Bolivia was defeated; tens of thousands died in the desert scrub, and the country lost most of the disputed territory. The humiliation discredited the ruling order, radicalised a generation of veterans, and set in motion the upheavals that would culminate in revolution.

events.title: War of the Pacific
events.summary: Chile defeats Bolivia and its ally Peru and annexes Bolivia's coast; Bolivia loses its Pacific province of Antofagasta and becomes landlocked.

events.title: Treaty confirms the loss of the coast
events.summary: A treaty between Bolivia and Chile formally cedes the Pacific coast to Chile, fixing Bolivia's landlocked status and a lasting national grievance.

events.title: The age of the tin barons
events.summary: Tin replaces silver as Bolivia's great export, making a handful of mine owners enormously rich while the indigenous mine workers labour in poverty.

events.title: The Chaco War with Paraguay
events.summary: Bolivia is defeated by Paraguay in a brutal war over the Gran Chaco, losing most of the disputed land and discrediting the old governing order.

pullquote.text: The loss of the sea, confirmed in 1904, left Bolivia landlocked, a wound the nation has never ceased to contest.
pullquote.attribution: On the War of the Pacific

eras.title: The National Revolution and Military Rule
eras.standfirst: The shock of the Chaco War gave birth to the National Revolution of 1952, which nationalised the tin mines, gave indigenous Bolivians the vote and the land, and remade the nation, before two decades of military dictatorship overtook it.
body: Defeat in the Chaco shattered faith in the old elite and gave rise to new political movements, above all the Revolutionary Nationalist Movement (MNR), which drew together middle-class reformers, miners and peasants. After the MNR's candidate Victor Paz Estenssoro was denied an electoral victory, a popular insurrection in April 1952, in which armed miners and workers defeated the army, swept the party to power. The Bolivian National Revolution of 1952 was one of the most far-reaching upheavals in Latin American history: the new government nationalised the great tin mines of the barons, granted universal suffrage that for the first time gave the vote to the indigenous majority, and in 1953 decreed a sweeping agrarian reform that broke up the great estates and distributed land to indigenous peasants.

The revolution transformed Bolivian society but could not master its economy. Falling tin prices, runaway inflation and dependence on foreign aid sapped the MNR governments through the 1950s and into the 1960s, even as Paz Estenssoro returned to the presidency. In 1964 the military, led by his own vice-president, overthrew him, ending the revolutionary era and opening a long period of armed-forces rule. The following year the Argentine-born revolutionary Ernesto Che Guevara was hunted down and killed by the Bolivian army while trying to ignite a guerrilla insurgency in the country's southeast.

From 1964 to 1982 Bolivia was governed by a succession of military regimes, some reformist but many repressive. The dictatorship of General Hugo Banzer in the 1970s and the especially violent, drug-tainted regime of General Luis Garcia Meza in 1980-1981 marked the darkest of these years, with political persecution, exile and killing. Mounting popular resistance, strikes and international pressure finally forced the armed forces to give way, and in October 1982 they handed power to a civilian Congress, which installed Hernan Siles Zuazo as president and restored constitutional democracy.

events.title: The National Revolution
events.summary: A popular insurrection brings the MNR and Victor Paz Estenssoro to power, launching reforms that reshape Bolivia.

events.title: Tin nationalised, suffrage and land reform
events.summary: The revolutionary government nationalises the tin mines, grants universal suffrage to the indigenous majority, and decrees a sweeping agrarian reform.

events.title: Military coup ends the revolution
events.summary: The armed forces overthrow Paz Estenssoro, ending the revolutionary era and beginning eighteen years of military rule.

events.title: Death of Che Guevara
events.summary: The revolutionary Ernesto Che Guevara is captured and killed by the Bolivian army while leading a failed guerrilla campaign in the southeast.

events.title: Return to democracy
events.summary: After years of military rule and the violent Garcia Meza regime, the armed forces cede power to Congress, which makes Hernan Siles Zuazo president.

figures.name: Victor Paz Estenssoro
figures.blurb: Founder and principal leader of the MNR and three times president of Bolivia, who led the 1952 National Revolution that nationalised the tin mines, extended the vote to the indigenous majority, and carried out a sweeping agrarian reform.

pullquote.text: The revolution nationalised the tin mines, gave the indigenous majority the vote, and broke up the great estates.

eras.title: Democracy and the Indigenous-Majority Era
eras.standfirst: Restored democracy weathered hyperinflation and unrest before the rise of Evo Morales, Bolivia's first indigenous president, whose long tenure ended in the disputed election and resignation of 2019; the MAS returned, then fractured, and in 2025 the country turned to the centre-right.
body: The democracy restored in 1982 was tested at once by economic catastrophe: by the mid-1980s Bolivia suffered some of the worst hyperinflation in world history. A returning Victor Paz Estenssoro imposed a drastic stabilisation programme that tamed inflation but brought painful austerity and the collapse of much of the state tin industry. Over the next two decades a series of governments pursued market reforms and privatisation, but the gains bypassed the indigenous majority, and rising discontent erupted in mass protests, the Cochabamba water war of 2000 and the gas war of 2003, which forced a president from office and opened the way for a new political force rooted in the indigenous and coca-growers' movements.

In December 2005 Evo Morales, an Aymara former coca-growers' union leader and head of the Movement Toward Socialism (MAS), won the presidency, and in January 2006 he became the first indigenous president in Bolivia's history. His government nationalised the hydrocarbons industry, channelled gas revenues into social programmes that sharply cut poverty, and in 2009 enacted a new constitution refounding the country as a plurinational state that recognised its indigenous peoples. Morales won repeated elections and presided over years of growth, but his determination to remain in power, including a run for a fourth term after a referendum had rejected it, drew growing opposition.

In the October 2019 election Morales was declared the winner amid allegations of fraud; an Organization of American States audit reported serious irregularities, weeks of protest followed, and after the police and army withdrew their support he resigned in November and went into exile, an outcome his supporters denounced as a coup and his opponents as a defence of the vote. An interim government gave way to fresh elections in 2020, which the MAS won decisively under Luis Arce. But Arce and Morales then fell into a bitter rivalry that split the party, and amid a deep economic crisis of fuel shortages and inflation the left was routed in 2025, when the centrist Rodrigo Paz won the presidency, ending nearly two decades of MAS dominance. In June 2024 the country had also weathered a brief, failed military uprising in La Paz, a reminder of how fragile its hard-won democratic order remains.

events.title: Hyperinflation and stabilisation
events.summary: Amid catastrophic hyperinflation, a returning Paz Estenssoro imposes a drastic stabilisation programme that ends the crisis but brings severe austerity.

events.title: Evo Morales takes office
events.summary: Evo Morales, leader of the MAS, becomes Bolivia's first indigenous president, beginning an era of indigenous-majority politics and hydrocarbon nationalisation.

events.title: Plurinational constitution
events.summary: A new constitution refounds Bolivia as a plurinational state recognising its indigenous nations and expanding their rights.

events.title: Disputed election and Morales's resignation
events.summary: After a contested October vote that an OAS audit found marred by irregularities, weeks of protest and the loss of military and police support lead Morales to resign and go into exile.

events.title: Return of the MAS under Arce
events.summary: Luis Arce wins the presidency for the MAS, returning the party to power a year after Morales's fall.

events.title: Turn to the centre-right
events.summary: Amid economic crisis and a split between Arce and Morales, the centrist Rodrigo Paz wins the presidency, ending nearly twenty years of MAS rule.

figures.name: Evo Morales
figures.blurb: Aymara former coca-growers' union leader and founder of the MAS who in 2006 became Bolivia's first indigenous president. He nationalised hydrocarbons, cut poverty and refounded the state as plurinational, but resigned in 2019 after a disputed election that split the country.

figures.name: Luis Arce
figures.blurb: Economist and former finance minister under Morales who led the MAS back to power in 2020, then broke with Morales in a rivalry that fractured the party amid a worsening economic crisis.

pullquote.text: In January 2006 Evo Morales became the first indigenous president in the history of a nation whose majority is indigenous.

figures.name: Antonio Jose de Sucre
figures.role: Liberator and first president of Bolivia
figures.blurb: Bolivar's chief lieutenant and victor of the Battle of Ayacucho, who liberated Charcas and became the first constitutionally elected president of the republic named for the Liberator.

figures.name: Tupac Katari
figures.blurb: Leader of the Aymara siege of La Paz during the great Andean rebellion of 1781, now honoured as a martyr and symbol of Bolivia's indigenous resistance.

figures.name: Evo Morales
figures.blurb: Founder of the MAS and Bolivia's first indigenous president from 2006, whose long tenure ended in the disputed election and resignation of 2019.
